title: practical english usage - Part 170
content:
Relative clauses usually follow the nouns that they modify (►  233).

7 subordinate         clauses      (who     ..., after.     .., if  ..., because         ..., Basic Clause Types    Section  19 grammar »216     questions: basic rules o       ell me  what Tell me what you want, (N  T  T                      do you want?) not use a question mark For details, ►    260.

In an indirect question, we  do not put an auxiliary before the subject, and we  do 7 indirect      questions:      Tell    me    when      you     are    leaving.

For questions like Who do you wish that you'd married?, ►    264.

So who did marry the Princess in the end?

Well, tell us - what did happen when your father found you?

answer.

But do can be used after a subject question word for emphasis, to insist on an not How many people work in your office? (     How many people do work...?) longest) Which type of battery lasts longest? (NOT    Which type of battery does last more . . . ?) not Which costs more - the blue one or the grey one? (     Which does cost More examples: What did she say? (What is the object.) - What happened? (What is the subject.) Who did you phone? (Who is the object.) - Who phoned? (Who is the subject.) normally used. Compare: When  who, which, what or whose is the subject (or part of the subject), do is not 6  Who      phoned?        / Who      did    you     phone       ?

Where are the President and his family staying? (     Where-are staying the This happens even if the subject is very long.

When was your reservation made? (     When was made your reservation?) not Is your daughter having a lesson today? (     Is having your daughter...?) not  s Is your mother coming tomorrow? (     I  coming your mother tomorrow?) Only the auxiliary verb goes before the subject, not the whole of the verb phrase.

5  only    auxiliary       verb    before     subject to go.. .?) not Did you go climbing last weekend? (     Did you went... ? OR   Did you not What does the boss want? (     What does the boss wants?) After do, we  use the infinitive (without to).

4 infinitive       after    do:   What      does      he   want?

Have you seen Jack? (     D  you have seen Jack?) not Can you tell me the time? (     Do you can tell me the time?) Do is not used together with other auxiliary verbs or with be.

3 do   not   used    with    other     auxiliaries grammar »217     negative structures: basic rules For the difference between not and no with nouns, ►    536.

It was not George that came, but his brother, (     Net George came-...) structure with it ('cleft sentence', ►    273).

We   do not usually begin a sentence with not + subject. Instead, we  use a It's working, but not well.

Ask Jake, not his wife. Come early, but not before six.

We   can put not with other parts of a clause, not only a verb.

4 other     parts    of  a  clause:    not    his    wife,     not    before       six The best thing about the weekend is not working.

It's important not to worry, (     .. .-te-denH-werry!) We   put not before infinitives and -ing forms. Do is not used.

3  infinitives       and   -ing    forms:    It's  important           not    to   worry.

Don't be rude.

Do not / don't is also used to make the negative imperative of be.

Don't worry - I'll look after you. (     Worry-net-...) not Do not expect quick results, (     Expect-not-.. .) Negative imperatives are made with do not / don't + infinitive (►  224).

2 imperatives:        Don't    worry.

For negative subjunctives (e.g. It's important that he not be disturbed), ►    232.2.

For negatives without do in older English (e.g. I like him  not), ►    318.10.

For the dialect form ain't, ►    337.4.

For negative forms of have, dare, need and used, see the entries on these verbs.

The supper isn't ready, (     The supper doesn't be ready!) Do is not normally used with be (even when be is not auxiliary).

You mustn't worry, (     You-dorFt must-worry!) Do is not used with another auxiliary verb.

not                                      or                                          or I didn't think, (     I didn't to think,     L-didnH-thinking  J-didnh-thought!) Do is followed by the infinitive without to.
